articles.headline: India Launches Phase II of National Green Hydrogen Mission, Eyes Global Leadership
articles.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet on January 1, 2026, officially launched Phase II of the National Green Hydrogen Mission, accelerating India's transition to a hydrogen-based economy.
• This phase focuses on establishing large-scale green hydrogen production hubs, developing a robust supply chain, and promoting export opportunities.
• Key initiatives include production-linked incentives (PLI) for electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production, alongside significant R&D investments.
• The mission aims to achieve a production capacity of 5 million metric tonnes (MMT) of green hydrogen per annum by 2030, reducing fossil fuel imports and creating job opportunities.
• Special emphasis is placed on developing infrastructure for hydrogen storage, transportation, and end-use applications in sectors like refining, fertiliser, steel, and heavy-duty transport.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy
Environmental pollution and degradation
Conservation, environmental impact assessment
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
articles.source: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy
prelimsPoints: National Green Hydrogen Mission launched Phase II on Jan 1, 2026.
Target: 5 MMT green hydrogen production by 2030.
Focus on PLI for electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production.
Aims to reduce fossil fuel imports by over Rs 1 lakh crore and abate 50 MMT annual GHG emissions by 2030.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's strategic vision for energy independence and climate action through the Green Hydrogen Mission.
Analyze the economic implications, including job creation, export potential, and reduction in import bills.
Evaluate the challenges in scaling up green hydrogen production and infrastructure in India.
Examine the role of government policies like PLI in fostering domestic manufacturing and technology adoption.
articles.imageDescription: Illustration of green hydrogen production facility with wind turbines and solar panels.
articles.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the Universe, constituting roughly 75% of all baryonic mass.

articles.headline: Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 Comes into Full Effect Across India
articles.explainer:
• The Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP Act), 2023, is fully implemented nationwide from January 1, 2026, marking a significant milestone in India's digital governance.
• The Act establishes a comprehensive framework for processing digital personal data, ensuring the privacy rights of individuals (Data Principals).
• It mandates data fiduciaries to obtain explicit consent for data processing and outlines obligations for data protection and breach notification.
• The Data Protection Board of India (DPBI) is now fully operational to enforce the provisions, investigate complaints, and impose penalties for non-compliance.
• This legislation aims to balance individual data privacy with the need for legitimate data processing for innovation and economic growth.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Information Technology
prelimsPoints: DPDP Act, 2023 fully effective from Jan 1, 2026.
Establishes Data Protection Board of India (DPBI).
Mandates consent for data processing.
Applicable to processing of digital personal data within India and certain processing outside India.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the DPDP Act in safeguarding citizen's privacy in the digital age.
Discuss the challenges in implementing the Act, particularly for small and medium enterprises.
Evaluate the role of the Data Protection Board of India in ensuring compliance and addressing grievances.
Compare India's data protection framework with global standards like GDPR.
articles.imageDescription: Abstract image representing data privacy and digital security.
articles.trivia: India's Supreme Court, in the Puttaswamy judgment (2017), declared the Right to Privacy as a fundamental right under Article 21 of the Constitution.

articles.headline: India-EU Free Trade Agreement Negotiations Enter Final Phase Amidst Optimism
articles.explainer:
• Negotiations for the ambitious India-European Union Free Trade Agreement (FTA) have entered their final and critical phase, with both sides expressing optimism for an early conclusion in 2026.
• The latest round of talks, concluded in December 2025, saw significant progress on key areas including market access for goods and services, intellectual property rights, and sustainable development provisions.
• This FTA is expected to significantly boost bilateral trade and investment, reduce tariffs, and create a more predictable regulatory environment for businesses.
• Challenges remain on issues such as agricultural market access, non-tariff barriers, and digital trade rules, requiring continued high-level engagement.
• A successful agreement would position India as a key economic partner for the EU and provide Indian industries with greater access to the large European market.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: India-EU FTA negotiations in final phase on Jan 1, 2026.
Covers goods, services, IPR, sustainable development.
Aims to boost bilateral trade and investment.
EU is one of India's largest trading partners.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic and economic significance of the India-EU FTA for India's foreign policy and trade ambitions.
Discuss the potential benefits and challenges for various Indian sectors, including agriculture, manufacturing, and services.
Examine how the FTA could impact India's geopolitical standing and its relations with other major trading blocs.
Evaluate the role of sustainable development clauses in modern FTAs.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and European Union intertwined, symbolizing cooperation.
articles.trivia: The EU is the world's largest trading bloc, and India is its 10th largest trading partner.

articles.headline: India's Q3 FY26 GDP Growth Projected at 7.2%, Maintains Robust Economic Trajectory
articles.explainer:
• The National Statistical Office (NSO) has released advance estimates projecting India's GDP growth for the third quarter of Financial Year 2025-26 (October-December 2025) at 7.2%.
• This sustained growth is primarily attributed to strong domestic demand, robust manufacturing output, and a rebound in the services sector.
• Government capital expenditure continues to be a key driver, alongside private investment picking up pace in select sectors.
• Inflation remains a monitored factor, though easing commodity prices and supply-side management have contributed to stability.
• The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) is expected to maintain a cautious stance, balancing growth impulses with price stability concerns in its upcoming monetary policy review.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Investment models.
articles.source: Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation
prelimsPoints: Q3 FY26 GDP growth projected at 7.2%.
NSO releases GDP estimates.
Drivers: Domestic demand, manufacturing, services, government capex.
RBI monitors inflation and growth.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to India's sustained economic growth in recent quarters.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities for maintaining this growth trajectory in the medium term.
Examine the role of government spending and private investment in driving economic expansion.
Evaluate the implications of global economic trends on India's growth outlook.
articles.imageDescription: Graph depicting India's GDP growth trend.
articles.trivia: India is projected to become the world's third-largest economy by 2030, surpassing Germany and Japan.

articles.headline: ISRO Accelerates Preparations for Chandrayaan-4 Lunar Mission
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) has intensified preparations for its ambitious Chandrayaan-4 mission, aiming for a more complex lunar exploration.
• Following the successes of Chandrayaan-2 and Chandrayaan-3, Chandrayaan-4 is envisioned to include advanced scientific instruments, potentially a rover with enhanced capabilities, and a sample return module.
• The mission aims to further investigate the lunar south pole region, focusing on water ice distribution, mineral composition, and seismic activity.
• International collaborations are also being explored to augment the mission's scientific objectives and technological capabilities.
• A successful Chandrayaan-4 would solidify India's position as a leading space power and contribute significantly to global lunar science.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Space technology
prelimsPoints: Chandrayaan-4 is ISRO's next lunar mission.
Focus on lunar south pole, water ice, mineral composition.
Potentially includes advanced rover and sample return module.
Builds on Chandrayaan-2 and Chandrayaan-3 successes.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolving objectives of India's lunar exploration program, from Chandrayaan-1 to Chandrayaan-4.
Analyze the scientific significance of exploring the lunar south pole, particularly concerning water ice.
Examine the socio-economic benefits and technological spin-offs from India's space missions.
Evaluate the role of international collaborations in advancing India's space endeavors.
articles.imageDescription: Artistic rendering of Chandrayaan-4 Lander and Rover on the lunar surface.
articles.trivia: Chandrayaan-3 made India the fourth country to successfully land on the Moon and the first to land near the lunar south pole.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Bench to Review Petitions on Electoral Bond Scheme
articles.explainer:
• A Constitution Bench of the Supreme Court is scheduled to commence hearings on a batch of petitions challenging the legality and transparency of the Electoral Bond Scheme.
• The petitioners argue that the scheme, which allows anonymous donations to political parties, violates the Right to Information and undermines electoral transparency.
• The Union Government has defended the scheme, citing donor privacy and combating black money in political funding.
• This review is crucial for electoral reforms in India, potentially impacting future political funding mechanisms and the integrity of democratic processes.
• The outcome could lead to significant changes in how political parties receive donations and enhance public scrutiny of financial contributions.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions.
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act.
prelimsPoints: SC Constitution Bench to hear petitions on Electoral Bond Scheme.
Petitions challenge anonymity and transparency.
Government defends scheme citing donor privacy.
Scheme introduced in 2017 and implemented in 2018.
mainsPoints: Analyze the arguments for and against the Electoral Bond Scheme concerning electoral transparency and donor privacy.
Discuss the impact of anonymous political funding on democratic processes and accountability.
Examine the role of the judiciary in ensuring fairness and transparency in elections.
Suggest alternative mechanisms for political funding that balance transparency with practical considerations.
articles.imageDescription: Supreme Court of India building with legal documents in foreground.
articles.trivia: The Electoral Bond Scheme was introduced through the Finance Act, 2017, and became operational in January 2018.

articles.headline: National Education Policy 2020 Implementation Reviewed, Focus on Foundational Literacy and Numeracy
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education held a high-level review meeting on January 1, 2026, to assess the progress of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 implementation across states.
• The review highlighted significant strides in integrating vocational education, promoting multilingualism, and restructuring curricula.
• A critical focus area remains achieving universal foundational literacy and numeracy (FLN) by 2027, with special programs like NIPUN Bharat being scaled up.
• Challenges include ensuring equitable access to digital infrastructure, training educators for new pedagogical approaches, and securing adequate funding from states.
• The Ministry reiterated its commitment to decentralized implementation, encouraging states to adapt NEP guidelines to their specific contexts.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 implementation reviewed on Jan 1, 2026.
Focus on Foundational Literacy and Numeracy (FLN) by 2027.
NIPUN Bharat Mission is a key program for FLN.
Promotes vocational education and multilingualism.
mainsPoints: Analyze the transformative potential of NEP 2020 in reforming India's education system.
Discuss the key successes and persistent challenges in implementing NEP 2020 across diverse states.
Evaluate the strategies adopted to achieve universal foundational literacy and numeracy.
Examine the role of technology and teacher training in the successful implementation of the policy.
articles.imageDescription: Students in a modern classroom setting, symbolizing the future of education.
articles.trivia: The National Education Policy 2020 replaced the 34-year-old National Policy on Education (NPE) of 1986.

articles.headline: QUAD Leaders Reaffirm Commitment to Free and Open Indo-Pacific at Virtual Summit
articles.explainer:
• Leaders from the Quadrilateral Security Dialogue (QUAD) — India, the United States, Japan, and Australia — held a virtual summit on January 1, 2026, reaffirming their commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific.
• The summit focused on enhancing cooperation in maritime security, critical and emerging technologies, climate action, and regional infrastructure development.
• Discussions included strategies to counter aggressive actions in the region and uphold international law, particularly in the South China Sea.
• New initiatives were announced to strengthen supply chain resilience and promote vaccine equity and health security across the Indo-Pacific.
• The QUAD continues to evolve as a crucial platform for promoting shared democratic values and regional stability.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: QUAD virtual summit held on Jan 1, 2026.
Members: India, US, Japan, Australia.
Focus: Free and Open Indo-Pacific, maritime security, tech, climate.
Aims to counter regional aggression and uphold international law.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic significance of the QUAD in shaping the geopolitical landscape of the Indo-Pacific region.
Discuss the key areas of cooperation within the QUAD and their implications for India's foreign policy.
Evaluate the challenges and criticisms faced by the QUAD as a security and economic grouping.
Examine how the QUAD's initiatives contribute to regional stability and development.
articles.imageDescription: Logos of the four QUAD nations arranged together.
articles.trivia: The idea of the QUAD was first mooted by Japanese Prime Minister Shinzo Abe in 2007.

articles.headline: India Unveils New National Cybersecurity Framework to Bolster Digital Defenses
articles.explainer:
• The Indian government launched a comprehensive National Cybersecurity Framework on January 1, 2026, aimed at strengthening the country's digital infrastructure against evolving cyber threats.
• The framework emphasizes a multi-stakeholder approach, involving government agencies, private sector, academia, and individual citizens in cybersecurity efforts.
• Key pillars include enhanced threat intelligence sharing, capacity building for cyber professionals, robust incident response mechanisms, and promoting cyber hygiene.
• It introduces stricter compliance requirements for critical infrastructure sectors and establishes a dedicated fund for cybersecurity research and development.
• This initiative is crucial for protecting India's digital economy, citizen data, and national security in an increasingly interconnected world.
gsSubTopics: Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security; money-laundering and its prevention.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: New National Cybersecurity Framework launched on Jan 1, 2026.
Emphasizes multi-stakeholder approach.
Focus on threat intelligence, capacity building, incident response.
Introduces stricter compliance for critical infrastructure.
mainsPoints: Discuss the increasing threats to India's cybersecurity landscape and the necessity of a comprehensive framework.
Analyze the key features and implementation challenges of the new National Cybersecurity Framework.
Examine the role of public-private partnerships and international cooperation in enhancing cyber resilience.
Evaluate the impact of this framework on critical infrastructure sectors and individual data security.
articles.imageDescription: Digital lock icon with lines of code, symbolizing cybersecurity.
articles.trivia: India's first National Cyber Security Policy was released in 2013.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Internal Security
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Challenges to internal security through communication networks

articles.headline: India Crosses 150 GW Renewable Energy Capacity, Solar Leads the Charge
articles.explainer:
• India has achieved a significant milestone, surpassing 150 GW of installed renewable energy capacity as of January 1, 2026, with solar power contributing the largest share.
• This achievement marks rapid progress towards India's ambitious targets of 500 GW non-fossil fuel electricity capacity by 2030.
• Government policies, including solar parks, rooftop solar schemes, and PLI for solar module manufacturing, have been instrumental in driving this expansion.
• Challenges remain in grid integration, storage solutions, and land acquisition for large-scale projects, which are being addressed through policy reforms.
• The expansion of renewable energy is crucial for India's energy security, climate commitments, and sustainable economic development.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy
Environmental pollution and degradation
Conservation, environmental impact assessment.
articles.source: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy
prelimsPoints: India's renewable energy capacity crosses 150 GW by Jan 1, 2026.
Solar power is the largest contributor.
Target: 500 GW non-fossil fuel capacity by 2030.
Government schemes like solar parks and PLI for modules are drivers.
mainsPoints: Analyze India's progress and challenges in expanding its renewable energy capacity.
Discuss the role of solar power in achieving India's climate targets and energy independence.
Examine the policy and technological interventions required to overcome grid integration and storage issues.
Evaluate the socio-economic benefits of transitioning to a renewable energy-dominated grid.
articles.imageDescription: Vast solar power farm stretching to the horizon.
articles.trivia: The International Solar Alliance (ISA), headquartered in Gurugram, India, is a treaty-based intergovernmental organization working to promote solar energy.

articles.headline: Government Releases Draft AI Ethics Framework for Public Consultation
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) has released a draft National Framework for Artificial Intelligence (AI) Ethics for public consultation on January 1, 2026.
• The framework proposes guidelines for responsible AI development and deployment, focusing on fairness, transparency, accountability, safety, and privacy.
• It aims to foster innovation in AI while mitigating potential risks such as algorithmic bias, job displacement, and misuse of AI technologies.
• The draft suggests establishing an AI Ethics Council to oversee compliance and provide recommendations for future policy interventions.
• This move signals India's proactive approach to govern AI, positioning itself as a leader in ethical AI development on the global stage.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
prelimsPoints: Draft AI Ethics Framework released by MeitY on Jan 1, 2026.
Focus on fairness, transparency, accountability, safety, privacy.
Proposes an AI Ethics Council.
Aims to balance AI innovation with risk mitigation.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ethical challenges posed by the rapid advancement of Artificial Intelligence and the need for a regulatory framework.
Analyze the key principles outlined in India's draft AI Ethics Framework and their potential impact on AI development.
Examine the role of government, industry, and civil society in ensuring responsible AI innovation.
Compare India's approach to AI ethics with international best practices and regulatory models.
articles.imageDescription: Abstract representation of AI brain with ethical guidelines overlaid.
articles.trivia: The term 'Artificial Intelligence' was coined by John McCarthy in 1956.

articles.headline: Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban 2.0 Shows Significant Progress in Waste Management
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs announced significant progress in the Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban 2.0 (SBM-U 2.0) on January 1, 2026, particularly in solid waste management and sanitation.
• The mission has achieved over 90% door-to-door waste collection efficiency in urban areas and a substantial increase in waste processing capacity.
• Focus has shifted towards circular economy principles, promoting waste-to-wealth initiatives, and enhancing plastic waste management.
• Challenges include behavioral change, financial sustainability of urban local bodies, and managing legacy waste sites.
• SBM-U 2.0 aims to make all Indian cities 'Garbage Free' and 'Water Secure' by 2026, contributing to public health and environmental sustainability.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Poverty and developmental issues, Urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
articles.source: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs
prelimsPoints: SBM-U 2.0 progress in waste management announced on Jan 1, 2026.
Achieved over 90% door-to-door waste collection.
Aims for 'Garbage Free' and 'Water Secure' cities by 2026.
Focus on circular economy and plastic waste management.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolution and achievements of the Swachh Bharat Mission in transforming urban sanitation and waste management.
Analyze the key strategies and innovations employed in SBM-U 2.0 to achieve its 'Garbage Free' city objective.
Examine the challenges in sustaining waste management initiatives and ensuring behavioral change at the community level.
Evaluate the role of urban local bodies and citizen participation in the success of the mission.
articles.imageDescription: Clean street in an Indian city with waste segregation bins.
articles.trivia: Swachh Bharat Mission was launched on October 2, 2014, to achieve universal sanitation coverage and eliminate open defecation.

articles.headline: New Report Highlights Accelerated Himalayan Glacier Melt, Urgent Climate Action Needed
articles.explainer:
• A new scientific report released on January 1, 2026, by a consortium of research institutes, warns of an accelerated rate of melting of Himalayan glaciers, posing severe threats to water security.
• The report, based on extensive satellite imagery and field studies, indicates that glaciers in the Hindu Kush Himalayan (HKH) region are losing mass at an unprecedented pace.
• This rapid melt directly impacts the water supply of major rivers, affecting billions of people downstream and increasing the risk of glacial lake outburst floods (GLOFs).
• The findings underscore the urgent need for enhanced regional cooperation on climate change mitigation and adaptation strategies.
• Scientists call for immediate global efforts to reduce greenhouse gas emissions to preserve these vital 'water towers' of Asia.
gsSubTopics: Environmental pollution and degradation
Conservation, environmental impact assessment
Disaster and disaster management.
prelimsPoints: Report on accelerated Himalayan glacier melt released on Jan 1, 2026.
Glaciers in Hindu Kush Himalayan (HKH) region are rapidly losing mass.
Impacts water supply of major rivers and increases GLOF risk.
Calls for urgent climate change mitigation and adaptation.
mainsPoints: Analyze the implications of accelerated Himalayan glacier melt for India's water security, agriculture, and disaster management.
Discuss the scientific evidence and methodologies used to monitor glacier changes in the HKH region.
Examine the policy responses required at national and regional levels to address the challenges posed by climate change in the Himalayas.
Evaluate the role of international cooperation in mitigating climate change impacts on transboundary river systems.
articles.imageDescription: Melting glacier in the Himalayas with a visible receding ice line.
articles.trivia: The Hindu Kush Himalayan region is often referred to as the 'Third Pole' due to its vast ice and snow reserves.

articles.headline: India Reiterates Call for UN Security Council Reforms as Non-Permanent Term Concludes
articles.explainer:
• As India concludes its two-year term as a non-permanent member of the UN Security Council (UNSC) on December 31, 2025, it reiterated its strong call for comprehensive reforms of the Council.
• India advocated for an expansion of both permanent and non-permanent categories to reflect contemporary global realities and enhance the Council's legitimacy and effectiveness.
• During its term, India championed issues such as counter-terrorism, maritime security, peacekeeping, and climate action, contributing significantly to global discourse.
• Challenges to UNSC reform include entrenched interests of existing permanent members and divergent views among member states on expansion modalities.
• India continues to push for a permanent seat, arguing for its demographic size, economic power, democratic credentials, and contributions to international peace and security.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: India concluded UNSC non-permanent term on Dec 31, 2025.
Reiterated call for comprehensive UNSC reforms.
Advocates for expansion of both permanent and non-permanent categories.
India served as non-permanent member for 2024-2025.
mainsPoints: Analyze India's persistent demand for UN Security Council reforms and its rationale for a permanent seat.
Discuss the challenges and complexities involved in reforming the UNSC, considering the interests of various member states.
Evaluate India's contributions and priorities during its non-permanent term in the UNSC.
Examine the broader implications of UNSC reform for global governance and multilateralism.
articles.imageDescription: United Nations headquarters building with Indian flag.
articles.trivia: India has served eight terms as a non-permanent member of the UN Security Council.

articles.headline: Pilot Urban Employment Guarantee Scheme Launched in Select Cities
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Labour and Employment, in collaboration with state governments, has launched a pilot Urban Employment Guarantee Scheme (UEGS) in 20 select cities on January 1, 2026.
• Modelled on the Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act (MGNREGA), the UEGS aims to provide guaranteed wage employment to urban poor and unemployed.
• The scheme focuses on creating durable public assets such as urban infrastructure, sanitation projects, and environmental conservation initiatives.
• It seeks to address urban unemployment, enhance livelihood security, and contribute to urban development.
• Initial challenges include identifying eligible beneficiaries, mobilizing resources, and ensuring effective implementation in diverse urban settings.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Poverty and developmental issues, Urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
prelimsPoints: Pilot Urban Employment Guarantee Scheme (UEGS) launched in 20 cities on Jan 1, 2026.
Modelled on MGNREGA.
Aims to provide wage employment to urban poor.
Focus on creating urban infrastructure and sanitation assets.
mainsPoints: Analyze the rationale and potential benefits of an urban employment guarantee scheme in addressing urban poverty and unemployment.
Discuss the key differences and similarities between UEGS and MGNREGA, and the lessons learned from rural implementation.
Examine the challenges in implementing UEGS, including funding, identification of beneficiaries, and creation of meaningful work.
Evaluate the potential impact of UEGS on urban infrastructure development and livelihood security.
articles.imageDescription: Urban construction site with workers, symbolizing employment generation.
articles.trivia: MGNREGA guarantees 100 days of wage employment in a financial year to every rural household whose adult members volunteer to do unskilled manual work.

articles.headline: Team of Scientists Discover New Species of Amphibian in Western Ghats
articles.explainer:
• A team of Indian herpetologists announced the discovery of a new species of frog in the biodiversity hotspot of the Western Ghats on January 1, 2026.
• The newly identified amphibian, provisionally named 'Ghatiana mirabilis', exhibits unique morphological and genetic characteristics, distinguishing it from known species.
• This discovery underscores the immense biodiversity of the Western Ghats, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, and the need for its continued conservation.
• The research highlights the importance of sustained exploration and documentation of flora and fauna in ecologically sensitive regions.
• Scientists emphasize that habitat loss and climate change continue to pose significant threats to such endemic species, urging for stricter conservation measures.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Biodiversity.
prelimsPoints: New frog species, 'Ghatiana mirabilis', discovered in Western Ghats on Jan 1, 2026.
Western Ghats is a biodiversity hotspot and UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Discovery highlights need for conservation.
Threats: habitat loss and climate change.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of biodiversity hotspots like the Western Ghats for global ecological balance.
Analyze the threats faced by endemic species in the Western Ghats and the effectiveness of current conservation efforts.
Examine the role of scientific research and documentation in informing biodiversity conservation policies.
Evaluate the impact of climate change on amphibian populations and ecosystems.
articles.imageDescription: Close-up photo of a newly discovered frog species in its natural habitat.
articles.trivia: The Western Ghats are older than the Himalayan mountains and are home to at least 325 globally threatened species.

articles.headline: ISRO Successfully Launches Geostationary Meteorological Satellite (GMS-1)
articles.explainer:
• ISRO successfully launched its next-generation Geostationary Meteorological Satellite (GMS-1) from Satish Dhawan Space Centre on January 1, 2026.
• GMS-1 is designed to provide high-resolution imaging of Earth's weather systems, monitor atmospheric phenomena, and enhance climate studies.
• Equipped with advanced sensors, it will significantly improve India's capability for weather forecasting, disaster warning, and agricultural advisories.
• The satellite will operate from a geostationary orbit, offering continuous coverage over the Indian subcontinent and surrounding regions.
• This mission reinforces India's self-reliance in space technology and its commitment to providing essential services for societal benefit.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Space technology.

GMS-1 is a geostationary meteorological satellite.
Aims for high-resolution weather imaging, atmospheric monitoring.
Will improve weather forecasting and disaster warning for India.
mainsPoints: Discuss the role of meteorological satellites in enhancing India's disaster preparedness and agricultural productivity.
Analyze the technological advancements in India's satellite capabilities, as demonstrated by GMS-1.
Examine the benefits of geostationary satellites for continuous monitoring of weather patterns.
Evaluate ISRO's contributions to societal development through its various space missions.
articles.imageDescription: Rocket launching into space with a satellite payload.
articles.trivia: India's first dedicated meteorological satellite, Kalpana-1, was launched in 2002.

articles.headline: Prime Minister Inaugurates New Dedicated Freight Corridor Section, Boosts Logistics Efficiency
articles.explainer:
• The Prime Minister inaugurated a new 250 km section of the Eastern Dedicated Freight Corridor (EDFC) on January 1, 2026, marking a significant step in modernizing India's railway infrastructure.
• This new section is expected to significantly reduce freight transit times, ease congestion on existing passenger lines, and lower logistics costs for industries.
• The Dedicated Freight Corridors (DFCs) project is a flagship initiative aimed at creating a robust and efficient freight transportation network across the country.
• It will primarily benefit industries in the northern and eastern regions, facilitating faster movement of raw materials and finished goods.
• The project utilizes advanced signaling systems and electrification, contributing to greener and more efficient freight operations.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Investment models.
prelimsPoints: New 250 km section of EDFC inaugurated on Jan 1, 2026.
Aims to reduce freight transit times and congestion.
DFCs are flagship projects for efficient freight network.
Benefits industries in northern and eastern regions.
mainsPoints: Analyze the economic significance of Dedicated Freight Corridors for India's industrial growth and logistics sector.
Discuss how DFCs contribute to enhancing the competitiveness of Indian manufacturing and exports.
Examine the challenges faced in the implementation and financing of large-scale infrastructure projects like DFCs.
Evaluate the environmental benefits of shifting freight from roads to electrified rail networks.
articles.imageDescription: Modern freight train on a dedicated track, symbolizing efficient logistics.
articles.trivia: India has two major Dedicated Freight Corridors: the Eastern DFC and the Western DFC.

articles.headline: ASI Launches Major Conservation Project for Ancient Buddhist Sites
articles.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) initiated a major conservation and restoration project for several ancient Buddhist sites across India on January 1, 2026.
• The project aims to preserve the structural integrity and historical authenticity of these sites, which are crucial for understanding India's rich Buddhist heritage.
• It includes extensive archaeological excavations, scientific conservation techniques, and improved visitor amenities.
• Special focus is on sites along the Buddhist Circuit, enhancing their appeal for both domestic and international tourists and pilgrims.
• This initiative is vital for promoting cultural tourism, preserving national heritage, and fostering a deeper understanding of India's historical contributions to global civilizations.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.

Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.
prelimsPoints: ASI launched conservation project for Buddhist sites on Jan 1, 2026.
Focus on structural integrity and historical authenticity.

mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of Buddhist heritage sites in understanding India's ancient history and cultural diplomacy.
Analyze the challenges involved in conserving ancient archaeological sites, including environmental factors and resource constraints.
Examine the role of the Archaeological Survey of India in protecting and promoting India's cultural heritage.
Evaluate the potential of cultural tourism, particularly the Buddhist Circuit, for regional development and employment generation.
articles.imageDescription: Ancient stupa at a Buddhist archaeological site.
articles.trivia: Sarnath, near Varanasi, is one of the most important Buddhist pilgrimage sites, where Buddha first taught the Dharma.

articles.headline: Brazil Assumes G20 Presidency, Outlines Agenda Focused on Social Equity and Climate
articles.explainer:
• Brazil officially assumed the G20 Presidency on January 1, 2026, inheriting the baton from South Africa and setting its agenda for the year.
• Brazil's presidency will focus on three key priorities: combating hunger and poverty, sustainable development, and global governance reform, particularly of multilateral institutions.
• The agenda emphasizes social equity, inclusive growth, and climate action, aiming to strengthen global cooperation on these pressing issues.
• India, as part of the G20 Troika, will play a crucial role in supporting Brazil's presidency and ensuring continuity in the G20's long-term objectives.
• The G20 summit in 2026 will be held in Rio de Janeiro, bringing together leaders from the world's major economies to address global challenges.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: Brazil assumed G20 Presidency on Jan 1, 2026.
Priorities: combating hunger/poverty, sustainable development, global governance reform.
India is part of the G20 Troika (previous, current, next president).
G20 summit 2026 to be held in Rio de Janeiro.
mainsPoints: Analyze Brazil's G20 presidency agenda and its alignment with global development challenges.
Discuss India's role as part of the G20 Troika and its influence on continuity and agenda setting.
Examine the significance of the G20 as a platform for addressing issues of global economic stability and development.
Evaluate the challenges in achieving consensus on critical issues like climate action and multilateral institution reform within the G20.
articles.imageDescription: G20 logo with flags of member countries, Brazil prominently featured.
articles.trivia: The G20 represents around 85% of global GDP, over 75% of global trade, and about two-thirds of the world population.

articles.headline: RBI Introduces New Regulatory Sandbox for Fintech Innovations
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) announced the launch of a new cohort for its Regulatory Sandbox on January 1, 2026, specifically targeting innovations in 'Green Finance' and 'Cross-border Payments'.
• This initiative aims to foster responsible innovation in the fintech sector by allowing entities to test new products, services, and business models in a live but controlled environment.
• The sandbox provides regulatory clarity, reduces time-to-market, and helps identify potential risks before wider deployment.
• Focus on 'Green Finance' aligns with India's climate goals, promoting sustainable financial products and services.
• 'Cross-border Payments' innovations seek to enhance efficiency and reduce costs in international transactions, benefiting trade and remittances.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: RBI launched new Regulatory Sandbox cohort on Jan 1, 2026.
Focus areas: 'Green Finance' and 'Cross-border Payments'.
Allows testing of new fintech products in a controlled environment.
Aims to foster responsible innovation and reduce time-to-market.
mainsPoints: Discuss the role of regulatory sandboxes in fostering innovation and managing risks in the financial technology sector.
Analyze the significance of 'Green Finance' and 'Cross-border Payments' as focus areas for India's fintech ecosystem.
Examine how such initiatives contribute to financial inclusion and economic efficiency.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for fintech startups operating within a regulatory sandbox framework.
articles.imageDescription: Digital circuit board with currency symbols, representing fintech.
articles.trivia: The term 'Fintech' is a portmanteau of 'financial technology'.

highlights: India launches Phase II of National Green Hydrogen Mission, targeting 5 MMT production by 2030, aiming for global leadership in green energy.
Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, comes into full effect, establishing a comprehensive framework for data privacy and setting up the Data Protection Board of India.
India-EU Free Trade Agreement negotiations enter the final phase, with optimism for an early conclusion to boost bilateral trade and investment.
ISRO accelerates preparations for Chandrayaan-4 lunar mission, envisioning advanced exploration and potentially a sample return from the lunar south pole.
Supreme Court Constitution Bench to review petitions challenging the legality and transparency of the Electoral Bond Scheme, crucial for electoral reforms.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the National Green Hydrogen Mission Phase II:
1. It aims to achieve a production capacity of 5 million metric tonnes (MMT) of green hydrogen per annum by 2030.
2. The mission focuses solely on domestic consumption, with no provisions for export promotion.
3. Production-linked incentives (PLI) are a key component, specifically for electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is correct. The mission aims for 5 MMT green hydrogen production by 2030. Statement 2 is incorrect; Phase II focuses on both domestic production and promoting export opportunities. Statement 3 is correct, PLI for electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production are key initiatives.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP Act), 2023, consider the following statements:
1. It mandates data fiduciaries to obtain explicit consent from individuals for processing their digital personal data.
2. The Data Protection Board of India (DPBI) is established under this Act to enforce its provisions.
3. The Act is applicable only to the processing of personal data within the territorial boundaries of India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statements 1 and 2 are correct. The Act mandates explicit consent and establishes the DPBI. Statement 3 is incorrect; the Act is applicable to processing of digital personal data within India and also to certain processing outside India if it is for offering goods or services to Data Principals in India.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following countries are members of the Quadrilateral Security Dialogue (QUAD)?
1. India
2. United States
3. Japan
4. South Korea
5. Australia
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: The Quadrilateral Security Dialogue (QUAD) comprises India, the United States, Japan, and Australia. South Korea is not a member of the QUAD.

prelimsMocks.question:
Regarding India's renewable energy capacity, consider the following:
1. India has crossed 150 GW of installed renewable energy capacity as of early 2026.
2. Solar power is the largest contributor to India's total renewable energy capacity.
3. India aims to achieve 500 GW of non-fossil fuel electricity capacity by 2030.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: All three statements are correct. India has achieved over 150 GW renewable capacity, with solar leading. The target for 2030 is 500 GW non-fossil fuel capacity.

prelimsMocks.question: The term 'Ghatiana mirabilis', recently in news, refers to a:
options: (b) Rare mineral found in the Eastern Ghats
(c) Newly identified species of amphibian in the Western Ghats
(d) Ancient script deciphered from Indus Valley Civilization
prelimsMocks.answer: (c) Newly identified species of amphibian in the Western Ghats
prelimsMocks.explanation: 'Ghatiana mirabilis' is the provisional name for a new species of frog discovered by Indian herpetologists in the Western Ghats, a biodiversity hotspot.

mainsMocks.question: Critically analyze the potential of India's National Green Hydrogen Mission (Phase II) to transform the country's energy landscape and contribute to global decarbonization efforts. What challenges must be addressed for its successful implementation? (15 marks, 250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Infrastructure: Energy, Environmental pollution and degradation, Conservation.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the mission and its objectives. Discuss its transformative potential for energy security, economic growth (PLI, jobs, exports), and climate action (decarbonization, reduced emissions). Then, outline key challenges such as technological scaling, infrastructure development (storage, transport), funding, skill development, and market demand creation. Conclude with the importance of a holistic approach.

mainsMocks.question: The full implementation of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, marks a significant step towards safeguarding citizen's privacy in the digital age. Discuss the key provisions of the Act and evaluate its implications for data fiduciaries and individual rights. (15 marks, 250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors; Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
mainsMocks.approach: Start with the context of the DPDP Act and its objective. Detail key provisions like explicit consent, obligations of data fiduciaries, rights of data principals (right to access, correction, erasure), and the role of the Data Protection Board of India (DPBI). Analyze implications for businesses (compliance, data governance, costs) and individuals (enhanced privacy, control over data, grievance redressal). Discuss challenges in implementation and enforcement.

mainsMocks.question: The QUAD grouping has emerged as a crucial platform for promoting a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific. Examine the strategic significance of QUAD for India's foreign policy and its contributions to regional security and cooperation. (10 marks, 150 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce QUAD and its members. Discuss its strategic significance for India in balancing regional power dynamics, promoting maritime security, and upholding international law in the Indo-Pacific. Highlight contributions to cooperation in critical technologies, climate action, and disaster relief. Conclude by acknowledging its evolving role and significance for India's multilateral diplomacy.

quickBytes.text: The National Green Hydrogen Mission (Phase II) aims for 5 MMT green hydrogen production by 2030.
The Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, is fully effective from January 1, 2026.
India's Q3 FY26 GDP growth is projected at 7.2%, driven by domestic demand and manufacturing.
Chandrayaan-4, ISRO's next lunar mission, may include a sample return module from the lunar south pole.
The Supreme Court's Constitution Bench is reviewing petitions challenging the Electoral Bond Scheme.
NIPUN Bharat Mission is a key initiative under NEP 2020 for Foundational Literacy and Numeracy.
India has surpassed 150 GW of installed renewable energy capacity, with solar as the largest contributor.
A new draft National Framework for AI Ethics has been released for public consultation.
Brazil assumed the G20 Presidency on January 1, 2026, focusing on social equity and climate.
The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) launched a new Regulatory Sandbox cohort for Green Finance and Cross-border Payments.

GS1.headline: ASI Launches Major Conservation Project for Ancient Buddhist Sites
GS1.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) initiated a major conservation and restoration project for several ancient Buddhist sites across India on January 1, 2026.
• The project aims to preserve the structural integrity and historical authenticity of these sites, which are crucial for understanding India's rich Buddhist heritage.
• It includes extensive archaeological excavations, scientific conservation techniques, and improved visitor amenities.
• Special focus is on sites along the Buddhist Circuit, enhancing their appeal for both domestic and international tourists and pilgrims.
• This initiative is vital for promoting cultural tourism, preserving national heritage, and fostering a deeper understanding of India's historical contributions to global civilizations.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.

Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.
prelimsPoints: ASI launched conservation project for Buddhist sites on Jan 1, 2026.
Focus on structural integrity and historical authenticity.
Includes excavations and improved visitor amenities.
Aims to promote cultural tourism along the Buddhist Circuit.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of Buddhist heritage sites in understanding India's ancient history and cultural diplomacy.
Analyze the challenges involved in conserving ancient archaeological sites, including environmental factors and resource constraints.
Examine the role of the Archaeological Survey of India in protecting and promoting India's cultural heritage.
Evaluate the potential of cultural tourism, particularly the Buddhist Circuit, for regional development and employment generation.
GS1.imageDescription: Ancient stupa at a Buddhist archaeological site.
GS1.trivia: Sarnath, near Varanasi, is one of the most important Buddhist pilgrimage sites, where Buddha first taught the Dharma.

GS2.headline: Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 Comes into Full Effect Across India
GS2.explainer:
• The Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP Act), 2023, is fully implemented nationwide from January 1, 2026, marking a significant milestone in India's digital governance.
• The Act establishes a comprehensive framework for processing digital personal data, ensuring the privacy rights of individuals (Data Principals).
• It mandates data fiduciaries to obtain explicit consent for data processing and outlines obligations for data protection and breach notification.
• The Data Protection Board of India (DPBI) is now fully operational to enforce the provisions, investigate complaints, and impose penalties for non-compliance.
• This legislation aims to balance individual data privacy with the need for legitimate data processing for innovation and economic growth.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Information Technology
prelimsPoints: DPDP Act, 2023 fully effective from Jan 1, 2026.
Establishes Data Protection Board of India (DPBI).
Mandates consent for data processing.
Applicable to processing of digital personal data within India and certain processing outside India.
mainsPoints: Analyze the significance of the DPDP Act in safeguarding citizen's privacy in the digital age.
Discuss the challenges in implementing the Act, particularly for small and medium enterprises.
Evaluate the role of the Data Protection Board of India in ensuring compliance and addressing grievances.
Compare India's data protection framework with global standards like GDPR.
GS2.imageDescription: Abstract image representing data privacy and digital security.
GS2.trivia: India's Supreme Court, in the Puttaswamy judgment (2017), declared the Right to Privacy as a fundamental right under Article 21 of the Constitution.

GS2.headline: India-EU Free Trade Agreement Negotiations Enter Final Phase Amidst Optimism
GS2.explainer:
• Negotiations for the ambitious India-European Union Free Trade Agreement (FTA) have entered their final and critical phase, with both sides expressing optimism for an early conclusion in 2026.
• The latest round of talks, concluded in December 2025, saw significant progress on key areas including market access for goods and services, intellectual property rights, and sustainable development provisions.
• This FTA is expected to significantly boost bilateral trade and investment, reduce tariffs, and create a more predictable regulatory environment for businesses.
• Challenges remain on issues such as agricultural market access, non-tariff barriers, and digital trade rules, requiring continued high-level engagement.
• A successful agreement would position India as a key economic partner for the EU and provide Indian industries with greater access to the large European market.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: India-EU FTA negotiations in final phase on Jan 1, 2026.
Covers goods, services, IPR, sustainable development.
Aims to boost bilateral trade and investment.
EU is one of India's largest trading partners.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic and economic significance of the India-EU FTA for India's foreign policy and trade ambitions.
Discuss the potential benefits and challenges for various Indian sectors, including agriculture, manufacturing, and services.
Examine how the FTA could impact India's geopolitical standing and its relations with other major trading blocs.
Evaluate the role of sustainable development clauses in modern FTAs.
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and European Union intertwined, symbolizing cooperation.
GS2.trivia: The EU is the world's largest trading bloc, and India is its 10th largest trading partner.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Bench to Review Petitions on Electoral Bond Scheme
GS2.explainer:
• A Constitution Bench of the Supreme Court is scheduled to commence hearings on a batch of petitions challenging the legality and transparency of the Electoral Bond Scheme.
• The petitioners argue that the scheme, which allows anonymous donations to political parties, violates the Right to Information and undermines electoral transparency.
• The Union Government has defended the scheme, citing donor privacy and combating black money in political funding.
• This review is crucial for electoral reforms in India, potentially impacting future political funding mechanisms and the integrity of democratic processes.
• The outcome could lead to significant changes in how political parties receive donations and enhance public scrutiny of financial contributions.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions.
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act.
prelimsPoints: SC Constitution Bench to hear petitions on Electoral Bond Scheme.
Petitions challenge anonymity and transparency.
Government defends scheme citing donor privacy.
Scheme introduced in 2017 and implemented in 2018.
mainsPoints: Analyze the arguments for and against the Electoral Bond Scheme concerning electoral transparency and donor privacy.
Discuss the impact of anonymous political funding on democratic processes and accountability.
Examine the role of the judiciary in ensuring fairness and transparency in elections.
Suggest alternative mechanisms for political funding that balance transparency with practical considerations.
GS2.imageDescription: Supreme Court of India building with legal documents in foreground.
GS2.trivia: The Electoral Bond Scheme was introduced through the Finance Act, 2017, and became operational in January 2018.

GS2.headline: National Education Policy 2020 Implementation Reviewed, Focus on Foundational Literacy and Numeracy
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education held a high-level review meeting on January 1, 2026, to assess the progress of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 implementation across states.
• The review highlighted significant strides in integrating vocational education, promoting multilingualism, and restructuring curricula.
• A critical focus area remains achieving universal foundational literacy and numeracy (FLN) by 2027, with special programs like NIPUN Bharat being scaled up.
• Challenges include ensuring equitable access to digital infrastructure, training educators for new pedagogical approaches, and securing adequate funding from states.
• The Ministry reiterated its commitment to decentralized implementation, encouraging states to adapt NEP guidelines to their specific contexts.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 implementation reviewed on Jan 1, 2026.
Focus on Foundational Literacy and Numeracy (FLN) by 2027.
NIPUN Bharat Mission is a key program for FLN.
Promotes vocational education and multilingualism.
mainsPoints: Analyze the transformative potential of NEP 2020 in reforming India's education system.
Discuss the key successes and persistent challenges in implementing NEP 2020 across diverse states.
Evaluate the strategies adopted to achieve universal foundational literacy and numeracy.
Examine the role of technology and teacher training in the successful implementation of the policy.
GS2.imageDescription: Students in a modern classroom setting, symbolizing the future of education.
GS2.trivia: The National Education Policy 2020 replaced the 34-year-old National Policy on Education (NPE) of 1986.

GS2.headline: QUAD Leaders Reaffirm Commitment to Free and Open Indo-Pacific at Virtual Summit
GS2.explainer:
• Leaders from the Quadrilateral Security Dialogue (QUAD) — India, the United States, Japan, and Australia — held a virtual summit on January 1, 2026, reaffirming their commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific.
• The summit focused on enhancing cooperation in maritime security, critical and emerging technologies, climate action, and regional infrastructure development.
• Discussions included strategies to counter aggressive actions in the region and uphold international law, particularly in the South China Sea.
• New initiatives were announced to strengthen supply chain resilience and promote vaccine equity and health security across the Indo-Pacific.
• The QUAD continues to evolve as a crucial platform for promoting shared democratic values and regional stability.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: QUAD virtual summit held on Jan 1, 2026.
Members: India, US, Japan, Australia.
Focus: Free and Open Indo-Pacific, maritime security, tech, climate.
Aims to counter regional aggression and uphold international law.
mainsPoints: Analyze the strategic significance of the QUAD in shaping the geopolitical landscape of the Indo-Pacific region.
Discuss the key areas of cooperation within the QUAD and their implications for India's foreign policy.
Evaluate the challenges and criticisms faced by the QUAD as a security and economic grouping.
Examine how the QUAD's initiatives contribute to regional stability and development.
GS2.imageDescription: Logos of the four QUAD nations arranged together.
GS2.trivia: The idea of the QUAD was first mooted by Japanese Prime Minister Shinzo Abe in 2007.

GS2.headline: Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban 2.0 Shows Significant Progress in Waste Management
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs announced significant progress in the Swachh Bharat Mission-Urban 2.0 (SBM-U 2.0) on January 1, 2026, particularly in solid waste management and sanitation.
• The mission has achieved over 90% door-to-door waste collection efficiency in urban areas and a substantial increase in waste processing capacity.
• Focus has shifted towards circular economy principles, promoting waste-to-wealth initiatives, and enhancing plastic waste management.
• Challenges include behavioral change, financial sustainability of urban local bodies, and managing legacy waste sites.
• SBM-U 2.0 aims to make all Indian cities 'Garbage Free' and 'Water Secure' by 2026, contributing to public health and environmental sustainability.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Poverty and developmental issues, Urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
GS2.source: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs
prelimsPoints: SBM-U 2.0 progress in waste management announced on Jan 1, 2026.
Achieved over 90% door-to-door waste collection.
Aims for 'Garbage Free' and 'Water Secure' cities by 2026.
Focus on circular economy and plastic waste management.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolution and achievements of the Swachh Bharat Mission in transforming urban sanitation and waste management.
Analyze the key strategies and innovations employed in SBM-U 2.0 to achieve its 'Garbage Free' city objective.
Examine the challenges in sustaining waste management initiatives and ensuring behavioral change at the community level.
Evaluate the role of urban local bodies and citizen participation in the success of the mission.
GS2.imageDescription: Clean street in an Indian city with waste segregation bins.
GS2.trivia: Swachh Bharat Mission was launched on October 2, 2014, to achieve universal sanitation coverage and eliminate open defecation.

GS2.headline: India Reiterates Call for UN Security Council Reforms as Non-Permanent Term Concludes
GS2.explainer:
• As India concludes its two-year term as a non-permanent member of the UN Security Council (UNSC) on December 31, 2025, it reiterated its strong call for comprehensive reforms of the Council.
• India advocated for an expansion of both permanent and non-permanent categories to reflect contemporary global realities and enhance the Council's legitimacy and effectiveness.
• During its term, India championed issues such as counter-terrorism, maritime security, peacekeeping, and climate action, contributing significantly to global discourse.
• Challenges to UNSC reform include entrenched interests of existing permanent members and divergent views among member states on expansion modalities.
• India continues to push for a permanent seat, arguing for its demographic size, economic power, democratic credentials, and contributions to international peace and security.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: India concluded UNSC non-permanent term on Dec 31, 2025.
Reiterated call for comprehensive UNSC reforms.
Advocates for expansion of both permanent and non-permanent categories.
India served as non-permanent member for 2024-2025.
mainsPoints: Analyze India's persistent demand for UN Security Council reforms and its rationale for a permanent seat.
Discuss the challenges and complexities involved in reforming the UNSC, considering the interests of various member states.
Evaluate India's contributions and priorities during its non-permanent term in the UNSC.
Examine the broader implications of UNSC reform for global governance and multilateralism.
GS2.imageDescription: United Nations headquarters building with Indian flag.
GS2.trivia: India has served eight terms as a non-permanent member of the UN Security Council.

GS2.headline: Pilot Urban Employment Guarantee Scheme Launched in Select Cities
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Labour and Employment, in collaboration with state governments, has launched a pilot Urban Employment Guarantee Scheme (UEGS) in 20 select cities on January 1, 2026.
• Modelled on the Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act (MGNREGA), the UEGS aims to provide guaranteed wage employment to urban poor and unemployed.
• The scheme focuses on creating durable public assets such as urban infrastructure, sanitation projects, and environmental conservation initiatives.
• It seeks to address urban unemployment, enhance livelihood security, and contribute to urban development.
• Initial challenges include identifying eligible beneficiaries, mobilizing resources, and ensuring effective implementation in diverse urban settings.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
Poverty and developmental issues, Urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
prelimsPoints: Pilot Urban Employment Guarantee Scheme (UEGS) launched in 20 cities on Jan 1, 2026.
Modelled on MGNREGA.
Aims to provide wage employment to urban poor.
Focus on creating urban infrastructure and sanitation assets.
mainsPoints: Analyze the rationale and potential benefits of an urban employment guarantee scheme in addressing urban poverty and unemployment.
Discuss the key differences and similarities between UEGS and MGNREGA, and the lessons learned from rural implementation.
Examine the challenges in implementing UEGS, including funding, identification of beneficiaries, and creation of meaningful work.
Evaluate the potential impact of UEGS on urban infrastructure development and livelihood security.
GS2.imageDescription: Urban construction site with workers, symbolizing employment generation.
GS2.trivia: MGNREGA guarantees 100 days of wage employment in a financial year to every rural household whose adult members volunteer to do unskilled manual work.

GS2.headline: Brazil Assumes G20 Presidency, Outlines Agenda Focused on Social Equity and Climate
GS2.explainer:
• Brazil officially assumed the G20 Presidency on January 1, 2026, inheriting the baton from South Africa and setting its agenda for the year.
• Brazil's presidency will focus on three key priorities: combating hunger and poverty, sustainable development, and global governance reform, particularly of multilateral institutions.
• The agenda emphasizes social equity, inclusive growth, and climate action, aiming to strengthen global cooperation on these pressing issues.
• India, as part of the G20 Troika, will play a crucial role in supporting Brazil's presidency and ensuring continuity in the G20's long-term objectives.
• The G20 summit in 2026 will be held in Rio de Janeiro, bringing together leaders from the world's major economies to address global challenges.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.

India is part of the G20 Troika (previous, current, next president).
G20 summit 2026 to be held in Rio de Janeiro.
mainsPoints: Analyze Brazil's G20 presidency agenda and its alignment with global development challenges.

GS3.headline: India Launches Phase II of National Green Hydrogen Mission, Eyes Global Leadership
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet on January 1, 2026, officially launched Phase II of the National Green Hydrogen Mission, accelerating India's transition to a hydrogen-based economy.
• This phase focuses on establishing large-scale green hydrogen production hubs, developing a robust supply chain, and promoting export opportunities.
• Key initiatives include production-linked incentives (PLI) for electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production, alongside significant R&D investments.
• The mission aims to achieve a production capacity of 5 million metric tonnes (MMT) of green hydrogen per annum by 2030, reducing fossil fuel imports and creating job opportunities.
• Special emphasis is placed on developing infrastructure for hydrogen storage, transportation, and end-use applications in sectors like refining, fertiliser, steel, and heavy-duty transport.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy
Environmental pollution and degradation
Conservation, environmental impact assessment
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
GS3.source: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy
prelimsPoints: National Green Hydrogen Mission launched Phase II on Jan 1, 2026.
Target: 5 MMT green hydrogen production by 2030.
Focus on PLI for electrolyser manufacturing and green hydrogen production.
Aims to reduce fossil fuel imports by over Rs 1 lakh crore and abate 50 MMT annual GHG emissions by 2030.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's strategic vision for energy independence and climate action through the Green Hydrogen Mission.
Analyze the economic implications, including job creation, export potential, and reduction in import bills.
Evaluate the challenges in scaling up green hydrogen production and infrastructure in India.
Examine the role of government policies like PLI in fostering domestic manufacturing and technology adoption.
GS3.imageDescription: Illustration of green hydrogen production facility with wind turbines and solar panels.
GS3.trivia: Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical substance in the Universe, constituting roughly 75% of all baryonic mass.

GS3.headline: India's Q3 FY26 GDP Growth Projected at 7.2%, Maintains Robust Economic Trajectory
GS3.explainer:
• The National Statistical Office (NSO) has released advance estimates projecting India's GDP growth for the third quarter of Financial Year 2025-26 (October-December 2025) at 7.2%.
• This sustained growth is primarily attributed to strong domestic demand, robust manufacturing output, and a rebound in the services sector.
• Government capital expenditure continues to be a key driver, alongside private investment picking up pace in select sectors.
• Inflation remains a monitored factor, though easing commodity prices and supply-side management have contributed to stability.
• The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) is expected to maintain a cautious stance, balancing growth impulses with price stability concerns in its upcoming monetary policy review.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Investment models.
GS3.source: Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation

NSO releases GDP estimates.
Drivers: Domestic demand, manufacturing, services, government capex.
RBI monitors inflation and growth.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to India's sustained economic growth in recent quarters.
Discuss the challenges and opportunities for maintaining this growth trajectory in the medium term.
Examine the role of government spending and private investment in driving economic expansion.
Evaluate the implications of global economic trends on India's growth outlook.
GS3.imageDescription: Graph depicting India's GDP growth trend.
GS3.trivia: India is projected to become the world's third-largest economy by 2030, surpassing Germany and Japan.

GS3.headline: ISRO Accelerates Preparations for Chandrayaan-4 Lunar Mission
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) has intensified preparations for its ambitious Chandrayaan-4 mission, aiming for a more complex lunar exploration.
• Following the successes of Chandrayaan-2 and Chandrayaan-3, Chandrayaan-4 is envisioned to include advanced scientific instruments, potentially a rover with enhanced capabilities, and a sample return module.
• The mission aims to further investigate the lunar south pole region, focusing on water ice distribution, mineral composition, and seismic activity.
• International collaborations are also being explored to augment the mission's scientific objectives and technological capabilities.
• A successful Chandrayaan-4 would solidify India's position as a leading space power and contribute significantly to global lunar science.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Space technology

mainsPoints: Discuss the evolving objectives of India's lunar exploration program, from Chandrayaan-1 to Chandrayaan-4.
Analyze the scientific significance of exploring the lunar south pole, particularly concerning water ice.
Examine the socio-economic benefits and technological spin-offs from India's space missions.
Evaluate the role of international collaborations in advancing India's space endeavors.
GS3.imageDescription: Artistic rendering of Chandrayaan-4 Lander and Rover on the lunar surface.
GS3.trivia: Chandrayaan-3 made India the fourth country to successfully land on the Moon and the first to land near the lunar south pole.

GS3.headline: India Unveils New National Cybersecurity Framework to Bolster Digital Defenses
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian government launched a comprehensive National Cybersecurity Framework on January 1, 2026, aimed at strengthening the country's digital infrastructure against evolving cyber threats.
• The framework emphasizes a multi-stakeholder approach, involving government agencies, private sector, academia, and individual citizens in cybersecurity efforts.
• Key pillars include enhanced threat intelligence sharing, capacity building for cyber professionals, robust incident response mechanisms, and promoting cyber hygiene.
• It introduces stricter compliance requirements for critical infrastructure sectors and establishes a dedicated fund for cybersecurity research and development.
• This initiative is crucial for protecting India's digital economy, citizen data, and national security in an increasingly interconnected world.
gsSubTopics: Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security; money-laundering and its prevention.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
prelimsPoints: New National Cybersecurity Framework launched on Jan 1, 2026.
Emphasizes multi-stakeholder approach.
Focus on threat intelligence, capacity building, incident response.
Introduces stricter compliance for critical infrastructure.
mainsPoints: Discuss the increasing threats to India's cybersecurity landscape and the necessity of a comprehensive framework.
Analyze the key features and implementation challenges of the new National Cybersecurity Framework.
Examine the role of public-private partnerships and international cooperation in enhancing cyber resilience.
Evaluate the impact of this framework on critical infrastructure sectors and individual data security.
GS3.imageDescription: Digital lock icon with lines of code, symbolizing cybersecurity.
GS3.trivia: India's first National Cyber Security Policy was released in 2013.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Internal Security
